text:
Lithium metal anodes (LMAs) generally require Coulombic efficiency above 95% for practical use, and most capacity loss is attributed to the thickening of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) during discharge and charge processes. Although extensive studies have been dedicated to the formation and growth of the SEI film, the detailed mechanisms of the SEI growth process are still poorly understood. The electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance (EQCM) technique is an ideal tool to determine the *in-operando* dissolution of the SEI, as it bridges the ambiguous dissolution behavior with experimental results. Based on the previously established ether-based electrolytes with high performance on LMA, a systematic study on dissolution behavior of their corresponding SEI films using *in-operando* EQCM technique was carried out, and the related influencing factors were analyzed in detail.

In situ SEI solubility and correlations with surface passivity and lithium metal battery cyclability identified by electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance (EQCM) The relationships between solubility of the Solid-Electrolyte Interphase (SEI), surface passivity of the electrode material, and long term cyclability of lithium metal batteries are elucidated. Data obtained with a battery EQCM that simultaneously enables operation of a functioning lithium metal battery are integrated with x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) to elucidate the connection between the solubility of SEI films and their chemical structure. The information obtained can be exploited in the development of high performance battery electrolytes to address capacity loss of lithium metal batteries and to facilitate battery life time during operating and stationary modes.
